data_flow_trace.original: Staker calls renewLockUntil(type(uint64).max - lockedUntil); overflow check passes; result-too-short check passes since type(uint64).max >= now + minLockup
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: renewLockUntil() now begins with an explicit MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION cap (line 35: uint64 public constant MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION = uint64(4 * 365 days) * 1_000_000;). At lines 461-463, any durationMicros > MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION reverts with Errors.ExcessiveLockupDuration. Only after this gate are the overflow check (466-469) and the >= now + minLockup check (472-476) evaluated.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: durationMicros must be <= MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION (~1.26e14 µs = 4 years). type(uint64).max - lockedUntil (~1.84e19 µs) is many orders of magnitude larger, so the cap fires first.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: The reported snippet shows only the overflow check and a 'TODO(yxia): overlock protection check' marker. The current code adds the MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION cap that the TODO referenced as missing.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: lockedUntil can only grow by at most MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION per call. Even with repeated calls, every increment is bounded by lockedUntil + MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION not overflowing (line 466-469 catches that), so practical lockedUntil stays in the human-time range. Setting lockedUntil to type(uint64).max in a single call is impossible because the cap blocks any durationMicros large enough to do so.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Each renewLockUntil call adds at most ~1.26e14 µs. To reach 2^64-1 µs (~1.84e19) the staker would need ~146,000 sequential calls, each succeeding the overflow check; long before that, accumulated lockedUntil would put lockedUntil + MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION over 2^64-1 and the overflow check (467-469) reverts.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Reported attack assumes a single oversized duration is accepted; the cap forbids that.

data_flow_trace.original: Staker calls unstake(amount); _addToPendingBucket creates bucket with bucket.lockedUntil = type(uint64).max
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _unstake still passes the current lockedUntil into _addToPendingBucket, and the bucket inherits that value (line 607). The mechanism itself is unchanged, but the precondition (lockedUntil being type(uint64).max) is unreachable due to step 1.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Bucket lockedUntil can never be set to a value above what renewLockUntil/addStake permit. addStake (lines 412-424) sets lockedUntil = max(current, now + minLockup), also bounded by realistic timestamps.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Code path is unchanged; only the upstream input is bounded.

data_flow_trace.original: withdrawAvailable returns 0 forever because threshold = now - unbondingDelay << type(uint64).max
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _getClaimableAmount uses threshold = now_ - unbondingDelay and binary searches for buckets with lockedUntil < threshold. This logic is intact, but since bucket.lockedUntil is now bounded near real timestamps + at most 4 years lockup, eventually now_ - unbondingDelay > bucket.lockedUntil and the bucket becomes claimable.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Once block.timestamp exceeds bucket.lockedUntil + unbondingDelay (a maximum of ~4 years + unbondingDelay after the unstake), funds are claimable.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Same code, but no longer permanently unreachable.

data_flow_trace.original: Owner cannot recover funds; pending buckets are immutable
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Owner still has no mechanism to mutate pending buckets. acceptStaker even guards against role rotation while pending withdrawals exist (line 287). The structural lack of an admin override is unchanged.

reasoning_chain: The reported high-severity bug relied on the absence of an upper bound in renewLockUntil — exactly the missing check the original 'TODO(yxia): overlock protection check' comment flagged. In the current code at src/staking/StakePool.sol:461-463, that protection is implemented: any durationMicros > MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION (4 years in µs) reverts with Errors.ExcessiveLockupDuration before the overflow and minLockup checks run. The exploit's first step (calling renewLockUntil(type(uint64).max - lockedUntil)) is therefore rejected immediately. Repeated incremental calls also cannot reach type(uint64).max: each call is capped at +4 years, and the overflow check (466-469) reverts as soon as lockedUntil + durationMicros would exceed 2^64-1. The downstream code paths (_unstake, _addToPendingBucket, _getClaimableAmount) are functionally unchanged but no longer feedable with an attacker-controlled type(uint64).max lockedUntil. Test test_RevertWhen_renewLockUntil_overflow at Staking.t.sol:872-879 explicitly confirms that renewLockUntil(type(uint64).max) reverts. The fix is structural and complete.

key_evidence.excerpt:
/// @notice Maximum allowed lockup duration (4 years in microseconds)
uint64 public constant MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION = uint64(4 * 365 days) * 1_000_000;
key_evidence.why_matters: Defines the cap that prevents overlock; ~1.26e14 µs is many orders of magnitude below 2^64-1.
First guard in renewLockUntil — directly rejects the attack input type(uint64).max - lockedUntil.
Belt-and-suspenders overflow guard ensures even a sequence of capped calls cannot wrap around to a tiny value or saturate to type(uint64).max.
Regression test asserts the exact attack input from the report reverts.

counterargument: One could argue MAX_LOCKUP_DURATION (~4 years) is itself unbounded if the staker chains many calls; however, lockedUntil + durationMicros cannot exceed 2^64-1 (line 467 guard), so chaining saturates at ~year 584942 only after ~146,000 successful tx. Even granting that, withdrawAvailable resumes returning the funds once block.timestamp > bucket.lockedUntil + unbondingDelay, so any practically-reachable lockedUntil becomes claimable in finite (human-scale) time. The 'permanent freeze' claim therefore depends on reaching type(uint64).max in a single (or near-single) action, which the cap prevents. A more nuanced counter would be that an attacker could still push lockedUntil up to ~4 years from now in one call and effectively freeze funds for that period — but this is the documented design of the 4-year max lockup, not a vulnerability.
